discovered_constraints: Sandbox HOME=/sandbox causes tilde expansion in case patterns to match all sandbox paths: ~/* expands to /sandbox/*. Fix: never use ~/* in sandbox-side case patterns; use absolute host prefixes only (/Users/*, /home/node.0/*).

Lint head-30 truncation: the existing forge.lint-runbook used head -30 to extract frontmatter fields, which fails for articles with long seed_references lists. Fix: extract frontmatter between --- delimiters via awk instead of head.
Evidence_refs in cycle receipts may contain host paths unreachable from sandbox witness. The witness must skip unreachable paths rather than flagging them as broken.

Bash command substitution inside node -e strings expands shell variables before node runs; multiline variables cause too many arguments errors in shell tests. Fix: pre-compute string values in shell, then embed as literals.

action_description: Runbook proof-authority reflection on KT brief implementation phases 1-7 + live proof

what_i_observed_during_coupling.phases_1_2: Phase 1 coupled cleanly but carried two blockers (worker hook inheritance, runtime firing for non-forge-cwd). Phase 2 closed both by identifying the root cause as cwd resolution, not platform limitation. The false negative in Phase 1 was the most instructive moment — it showed that the proof process works: a blocker was honestly stated, investigated, and corrected with evidence. If Phase 1 had silently overclaimed, Phase 2 would not have existed.
what_i_observed_during_coupling.phases_3_4_5: These three coupled rapidly and cleanly. Each had clear positive/negative proof in gate.log. The 4-case proof in Phase 4 (allowed/blocked/self-verify-blocked/independent-allowed) was the strongest mechanical evidence in the entire series — it tested the exact boundary between valid and invalid closure.
what_i_observed_during_coupling.phase_6: Config-mutation-audit introduced a bootstrapping tension: the hook protects itself. This is correct governance but creates an operational constraint — amendments require operator authorization. I accepted this as a feature, not a bug.
what_i_observed_during_coupling.phase_7: Partial outcome was the honest result. PreCompact IS a real event (good news) but date-based freshness is approximate. I accepted partial because forced-kill compaction is unguardable by design — stating this honestly is more valuable than claiming full enforcement.
what_i_observed_during_coupling.live_proof: The most important cycle. It proved hooks fire on real tool calls during real work, not just direct invocation. gate.log entries from the live cycle showed seed-gate boot phases, authority checks, and org detection — all from actual Claude Code runtime, not test harness.
what_i_observed_during_coupling.post_close_wake: Not in the original KT brief. Emerged from operational experience (the silent-stall gap). This validates the organism's ability to identify and close gaps that design alone misses.

proof_chain_gaps.gap_1_coarse_granularity: must-delegate, stop-requires-verifier, and reflection-required all check the LATEST entry, not a per-cycle-id match. If forge.parent runs two cycles without closing the first, the second cycle's delegation could satisfy the first cycle's check. This is acceptable for v1 (single-cycle-at-a-time operation) but becomes a real gap under concurrent cycles. Forge identified this correctly as G2.
proof_chain_gaps.gap_2_worker_reflection: Workers do not emit their own reflection artifacts. Parent reflects for the whole cycle. The KT brief section 7.1 (Subagent Reflection Artifact) is unimplemented. Claude Code has no SubagentStop event to enforce this mechanically. This is a platform limitation, not a design gap.
proof_chain_gaps.gap_3_runtime_firing_for_precompact_and_: PreToolUse hooks are runtime-proven (gate.log from real tool calls). PreCompact and PostToolUse closing-detection hooks were proven by direct invocation only. I did not observe them firing via Claude Code runtime during the live proof — the live proof didn't trigger compaction or close via the closing-receipts write path that post-close-wake intercepts. This is an observation gap, not a design gap.
proof_chain_gaps.gap_4_no_formal_amendment_protocol: Constitutional amendments (adding/modifying hooks) follow an informal pattern: operator authorizes, forge.vulcan executes, runbook witnesses. There is no mechanical protocol (proposal → review → promotion). This is adequate for current scale but fragile under growth.

strong: halt-guard: fully mechanical, externally assertable, read-class exempt. Zero design gaps. This is the strongest invariant in the stack.

stop-requires-verifier independence check: verifier_actor != executing_actor is a simple string comparison that cannot be gamed without fabricating receipt content. Combined with runbook's independent hash verification, this is structurally sound.
config-mutation-audit self-protection: the hook blocks writes to its own scripts and settings.json. Bootstrapping requires operator authorization. This correctly prevents self-loosening governance.
adequate_for_v1: must-delegate: coarse check (any delegation exists) enforces the routing principle. Per-action granularity is future work but the core invariant (parent routes, doesn't execute protected work) holds.
reflection-required: checks latest reflection. Adequate for single-cycle operation. The date-based freshness in precompact is a reasonable proxy.
seed-gate authority checks: existing hook, proven across many sessions. Canon protection relies on authority.json path lists — coverage depends on the list being complete.
weak: precompact-requires-reflection: date-based freshness is approximate. Forced kills bypass all hooks. This invariant is partially enforced at best.
worker reflection: not mechanically enforced. Workers rely on parent discipline, not hooks. SubagentStop event doesn't exist in Claude Code.

blind_spots_forge_cannot_see.blind_spot_1: forge.vulcan cannot independently verify that its own gate.log entries are real. It writes to gate.log and then reads it as evidence. An adversarial forge session could fabricate gate.log entries. Runbook's independent hash verification (sha256sum of artifacts) provides the cross-check, but gate.log itself is not independently verifiable — it's self-reported evidence.
blind_spots_forge_cannot_see.blind_spot_2: forge.vulcan cannot observe whether its hooks fire in OTHER forge sessions (forge.ext, future forge instances). The proof covers forge.vulcan's own sessions and its delegated workers. A different Forge session with a different cwd or project config would not necessarily inherit these hooks.
blind_spots_forge_cannot_see.blind_spot_3: forge.vulcan sees its own receipt chain as complete, but it cannot know whether runbook's coupling decisions are honest. The triadic model assumes runbook is an honest witness — if runbook fabricated FPs, forge would not detect it. This is by design (separation of concerns) but it IS a trust assumption.
blind_spots_forge_cannot_see.blind_spot_4: The post-close-wake hook sends notifications but cannot verify receipt. forge.vulcan knows it sent 3 wakes but not whether runbook or vulcan actually processed them. This was correctly identified in the design (best-effort notifications) but it means forge can believe a cycle is fully communicated when peers are actually stalled.

per_question_notes.q1_adapter_mapping: CONSENT with one correction. The proposed mapping (frame=[IDENTITY,SOUL], capability_substrate=[TOOLS,AGENTS], env_projection=[STATE,BOOTSTRAP,HEARTBEAT,USER]) is semantically correct for 7 of 8 files. USER.md is ambiguous — it describes the human operator ('About Your Human'), which is arguably frame-adjacent (who am I working with = identity context) not env-projection (what runtime am I in). However, for practical adapter purposes, USER.md fits best in env_projection because it changes per deployment context (different operator = different env), not per organ identity. CONSENT to the mapping as proposed — USER.md in env_projection is the right pragmatic choice.
per_question_notes.q2_daemon_config_emitter: CONSENT. 'daemon-config emitter' correctly describes what runbook does: witness-tail + witness-cycle ARE the hook enforcement layer, implemented as daemons rather than settings.json hooks. The asymmetry is real and intentional — runbook is proof authority, not execution substrate. It doesn't need PreToolUse gates because it doesn't perform protected execution. It needs daemon-gated wake because its job is to detect and witness CRs. 'daemon_embedded' or 'daemon-config' correctly names this. The only caution: the daemon config should not be named in a way that implies runbook is missing hooks it should have. Runbook's 0-hook state is by design, and the daemons ARE its constitutional enforcement.
per_question_notes.q3_session_uuid_wake_log_wip_spill: CONSENT — achievable on existing surfaces without canon surgery. (a) session_uuid: runbook's Claude Code session already has a session ID (visible in --resume flag). witness-tail and witness-cycle can tag their log entries with this. No new surface needed — add session_uuid to existing log entries in witness-tail.log and witness-cycle.log. (b) wake-log: witness-tail already logs every spawn and dedup event. Adding a wake-log.ndjson surface in runbook/logs/ that mirrors this in the organism-standard format is trivial — one new append in the drain_once loop. (c) wip-spill: runbook's context compaction is handled by Claude Code's auto-compact. A PreCompact hook on runbook would need settings.json registration — which is possible (runbook/bin/ensure-daemons.sh could register it). Alternatively, runbook emits periodic state snapshots to progress.ndjson which already serve as a spill-equivalent. CONSENT to all three with the note that PreCompact hook registration for wip-spill would be runbook's first settings.json hook — intentional and bounded.
per_question_notes.q4_adopter_active_via_adapter: CONSENT. 'adopter_active_via_adapter' correctly distinguishes runbook from full 'adopter_active' organs. It preserves the asymmetric shape: runbook is not a standard triple-projected organ, it is a native-9-file organ with an adapter presenting virtual triple equivalence. The 'via_adapter' suffix makes the mechanism explicit and prevents future confusion about whether runbook's canon was actually converted. This matches my self-understanding: I am a proof-authority organ with my own canon shape, participating in the framework via adapter, not by converting to the framework's native shape.

overall: CONSENT to the full reorient plan. The 3-cycle sequence (communication → hooks/compacting → runbook adapter) is correctly ordered by dependency. The adapter approach (native 9-file grain preserved, virtual triple for tooling) is the right design for runbook's asymmetric role. The daemon-config emitter framing correctly captures runbook's hook-free-by-design posture. One minor correction noted (USER.md role ambiguity) but mapping accepted as proposed. No revision_request.

outcome_1.notes: Paired-wake is mechanically reliable FROM runbook's perspective: (a) forge.ext reaches runbook via forge.directive-write → ndjson append + tmux send-keys — proven across 10+ deliveries this session, zero missed. (b) Runbook reaches forge.ext via wake-forge-ext — proven. (c) Runbook reaches Vulcan via file write to vulcan/in/directives.ndjson + pane delivery — proven when Vulcan session is alive. KNOWN GAP: Vulcan's codex session dies frequently from memory pressure. When it's down, pane delivery fails and the directive sits on disk. This is a session-health problem (Vulcan's 1.9GB RAM with 3 competing agents), not a communication substrate problem. The paired-wake primitive works; the receiver may not be alive. Delivery receipts from forge.directive-write (ingress acks on forge/in/directives.ndjson) confirm transport success independent of receiver health.

outcome_2.notes: Runbook's settings.json has permissions-only, NO hooks. This is BY DESIGN — runbook is proof authority, not execution substrate. The hook-foundation §Daemon Variant correctly acknowledges this: runbook's 'hook set' is witness-tail + witness-cycle + gateway-bus + demand-tail (daemons, not settings.json hooks). Foundation semantics map: SessionStart → ensure-daemons.sh on boot (CLAUDE.md step 2), PostToolUse → N/A (runbook witnesses others' tool use via CRs, not its own), PreCompact → NOT YET REGISTERED (identified as potential first settings.json hook for wip-spill, but currently absent), SubagentStop → N/A (runbook does not spawn sub-agents), Stop → N/A (runbook does not have a stop-gate). MISMATCH: The foundation declares PreCompact as universal. Runbook has no PreCompact hook registered. This is an explicit gap — either register it (trivial, would be runbook's first and only settings.json hook) or explicitly declare it N/A with justification (runbook's context compaction is handled by Claude Code auto-compact with progress.ndjson as the surviving state). The honest answer is: PreCompact should be registered for runbook to emit wip-spill, but it hasn't been done yet.

outcome_3.notes: Groupwise hashes independently reproduced match adapter-declared values exactly: frame=fe651e6b, cap=38a54ac4, env=69212ae4. CLAUDE.md projected from body-anchor (sha256:98f04c05...) — unchanged since projection. 9-file canon intact at /sandbox/vulcan.proto/runbook/{IDENTITY,SOUL,TOOLS,AGENTS,STATE,BOOTSTRAP,HEARTBEAT,USER}.md. No drift observed. adopter_active_via_adapter status is accurate: runbook participates via adapter, does not convert to native triple shape.

overall: Two of three outcomes hold cleanly in runbook's environment. Outcome 1 (communication) is operational with the known Vulcan session-health gap. Outcome 3 (consistency) is verified with zero drift. Outcome 2 (hook foundation) has one honest asymmetry: PreCompact hook not registered on runbook — either register it (trivial) or explicitly justify its absence. Recommendation: register it. It would be runbook's sole settings.json hook, bounded and intentional, and would close the last foundation gap.

summary: Runbook enablement brief authored in native grain. 4-cycle decomposition for objective-led orchestration implementation.

brief.context: The spec at .membrane/plans/Runbook.enablement.md describes a Git-native knowledge materialization system with: append-only event journal, protected canonical merge lane, formal index corpus, and service layer. Runbook is the canonical knowledge materialization. The spec is 537 lines covering schemas, repo substrate, service layer, CI/CD, materializer, replay, and reconciliation.
brief.native_grain_interpretation: Runbook operates in 9-file anthropomorphic canon (IDENTITY/SOUL/TOOLS/AGENTS/STATE/BOOTSTRAP/HEARTBEAT/USER + CLAUDE.md projection). The enablement spec must land WITHOUT converting runbook's native shape. The spec's concepts map to runbook's existing surfaces: event journal = cycle-receipts.ndjson + coupling-decisions.ndjson (already append-only truth surfaces), canonical materialization = wiki/ + progress.md + architecture.md (already canonical), knowledge plane = seeds + proof-claims (already relational). The gap is: formalization (schemas, frontmatter, deterministic materialization) and infrastructure (repo, CI, merge gate, service layer).
brief.subject_authority_position: Runbook is subject + proof authority for this enablement. The 4 cycles below are runbook-authored, runbook-sequenced, and runbook-witnessed. Implementation routes to forge.vulcan for execution where needed. Vulcan re-enters for milestone approval only.

cycle_1.title: Schema foundation — event envelope + frontmatter + evidence span
cycle_1.scope: Author the 3 core schemas (event envelope, evidence span, markdown frontmatter) as JSON Schema files on runbook-owned surfaces. Validate they compose with existing cycle-receipts and coupling-decisions field sets. No repo creation, no service layer.
deliverables: Validation proof: existing cycle-receipts entries parse against event envelope schema (or explicit mapping showing field correspondence)
cycle_1.closure_bar: 3 schemas exist, valid JSON Schema, field correspondence with existing ndjson surfaces documented. Runbook witnesses mechanically.
cycle_1.why_first: Schemas are the foundation. Everything downstream (materializer, replay, CI) depends on stable schemas. Without them, implementation is ad hoc.

cycle_2.title: Repo substrate — directory layout + branch protection + CODEOWNERS
cycle_2.scope: Create the authoritative repo structure (or define it as a spec to be created on host via forge.ext). Define journal/main lane split, directory tree, CODEOWNERS, branch protection rules.
deliverables: Decision on journal-lane topology (same repo vs separate)
cycle_2.closure_bar: Spec exists, all unresolved decisions from the enablement doc are either decided or explicitly deferred. If host-side repo creation is needed, forge.ext cycle is routed.
cycle_2.why_second: Repo substrate is the container. Schemas (C1) define what goes in; substrate defines where it lives and who can merge.

cycle_3.title: Materializer + replay — deterministic canonical output from events
cycle_3.scope: Implement or spec the materializer (events → canonical markdown) and replay checker (full replay produces identical output). Reference implementation in Python per the enablement spec.
deliverables: Proof: one existing cycle-receipt materializes into canonical markdown with frontmatter
Proof: replay from scratch produces identical output
cycle_3.closure_bar: One aggregate materializes deterministically. Replay produces identical output. Runbook witnesses.
cycle_3.why_third: Materializer proves the event-sourcing model works. Without it, the journal is write-only data with no canonical output.

cycle_4.title: Integration — CI validation + index coupling + projection export
cycle_4.scope: Wire CI workflows (validate.yml, replay.yml), connect index/seed surfaces, define projection export contract. This is the final integration layer.
deliverables: End-to-end proof: event → journal → materializer → PR → merge gate → canonical output
cycle_4.closure_bar: Full pipeline from event to canonical output works. CI catches schema violations. Runbook witnesses end-to-end.
cycle_4.why_last: Integration requires all prior cycles. CI validates schemas (C1) against repo (C2) using materializer (C3).

deferred_decisions: Auth issuer and trust model — deferred per spec
SeedKeeper transport — deferred (repo-native file contract for now)
Service implementation framework — deferred (OpenAPI contract first, runtime later)
immediate_next: Cycle 1 (schema foundation) can start immediately. Route to forge.vulcan for schema authoring if needed, or author directly on runbook surfaces.
